macro_segments.title: The Debate of Beit Shammai and Beit Hillel: Which Offering Has a Higher Minimum

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Debate: Higher Minimum for Olat Re'iyah or Shalmei Chagigah
The Philosophical Core of the Dispute: Divine vs. Human Benefit
Beit Hillel's Proof from Shlamim Before Matan Torah
Beit Shammai's Counter-Argument: Both Olah and Shlamim Existed
The Key Question: Was the Olah an Olat Re'iyah or Olat Tamid

macro_segments.title: Broader Tannaitic Debate: Three Against Three on the Identity of the Wilderness Offering

micro_segments.title: Abaye's Alignment: Beit Shammai, Rabbi Eliezer, and Rabbi Yishmael Hold for Olat Re'iyah
The Opposing View: Beit Hillel, Rabbi Akiva, and Rabbi Yose HaGlili Hold for Olat Tamid
Rabbi Yishmael's Theory of Torah Transmission: Klalot at Sinai, Pratim at Ohel Moed
Rabbi Akiva's Counter-Theory: All Details Given at Sinai, Only Repeated Later
The Philosophical Difference: Human License to Derive Details vs. Explicit Specification

macro_segments.title: Applying the Transmission Theory: How the Olat Tamid Could Be Brought Without Full Details

micro_segments.title: The Problem: If Details Were Only Given Later, How Could Olat Tamid Be Brought Before?
Tosafot's Solution: The Tamid Is Explicitly Mentioned Later, Making Earlier Sacrifice Inconceivable
Rabbi Elazar's Position: The Commandment Given at Sinai, but Not Executed Until Later
Rabbi Akiva Contra: The Olat Tamid Was Both Commanded and Executed at Sinai
The Rhetorical Question Resolved: Did They Bring Sacrifices All Forty Years?

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yose HaGlili's Position: Implicit Agreement That Olat Tamid Existed Before, Without Full Details

micro_segments.title: Three Mitzvot on the Regalim: Re'iyah, Chagigah, and Simchah, Each with Unique Aspects
Chagigah's Unique Status: It Existed Before Matan Torah, Unlike Re'iyah and Simchah
Implicit Conclusion: If Chagigah Alone Existed, the Olah Must Be Olat Tamid

macro_segments.title: Resolving the Alignment: Rabbi Yishmael Moves to the Beit Hillel Side

micro_segments.title: The Challenge to Rabbi Yishmael's Position: Can Olat Tamid Be Brought Without Full Specifications?
Rabbi Yose HaGlili's Precedent: Explicit Baraita Showing Change in Tamid Practice Over Time
Gemara's Conclusion: Rabbi Yishmael's Theory Permits Change; He Aligns with Beit Hillel

macro_segments.title: Textual Analysis: How to Parse the Verse About Sacrifices at the Foot of Sinai

micro_segments.title: Rav Chisda's Question: Does the Text Have a Period After Olot, Separating Olot from Shlamim?
Mar Zutra's Answer: The Resolution Depends on the Tropp (Cantillation Marks)
Rav Acha's Alternative: A Vow Formula to Determine If the Offering Was Sheep or Cattle
Unresolved Question: No Clear Answer About the Type of Animal

macro_segments.title: Introduction to Re'iyah as a Mitzvah Without Minimum or Maximum (At the Torah Level)

micro_segments.title: Mishnaic Teaching: Certain Mitzvot Have No Shiur (Limit); Re'iyah Is Among Them
Rabbi Yochanan's Initial Reading: No Upper Limit, But There Is a Lower Limit
Rabbi Oshaia's Correction: No Lower Limit Either at the Torah Level; Sages Added a Minimum

macro_segments.title: Definition of Re'iyah: Appearing Before God, with or Without a Korban

micro_segments.title: Dispute on the Meaning of Re'ayon: Rabbi Yochanan vs. Reish Lakish
Rabbi Yochanan's View: Re'iyah Means Appearing in the Azarah (Temple Court); No Korban Required Each Time
Reish Lakish's View: Re'iyah Always Means Bringing a Korban; Velo Yira'u Panai Reikam Applies Each Instance

macro_segments.title: Limiting the Dispute: First Day of Yom Tov vs. Other Days

micro_segments.title: Common Ground: On the First Day, Everyone Agrees You Must Bring a Korban Each Time You Appear
The Actual Dispute: On Other Days of Yom Tov, Can You Appear Without a Korban?
Rabbi Yochanan: On Other Days, Appearing Without Korban Is Permitted; No Gift Required
Reish Lakish: Every Appearance, Even on Other Days, Requires a Korban

macro_segments.title: Reish Lakish's Proofs: Deriving the Type of Korban from the Term Zevachim

micro_segments.title: Velo Yira'u Panai Reikam Cannot Be Fulfilled on Chol HaMoed Without a Korban, Says Reish Lakish
Logical Derivation (Dinah V'Omer): Chagigah and Re'iyah Are Parallel; Both Require Zevachim
Chagigah Must Be a Mammal: It Has Fat, So Re'iyah Korban Must Also Be a Real Animal
Further Derivation: Chagigah Is a Shelamim (You Eat It); Re'iyah Is an Olah (All to God)
Your Table Full, God's Table Empty: Anthropomorphic Reasoning for Why Re'iyah Must Be Olah

macro_segments.title: Reish Lakish's Counterargument: Limitation to First Day of Yom Tov

micro_segments.title: Reish Lakish Challenged on Chol HaMoed: Can You Really Come Empty-Handed Then?
Response: The Velo Yira'u Panai Reikam Prohibition Applies Only on the First Day (Ikar HaRegel)
